title: AI-Enabled Healthcare Workforce Planning Readiness Assessment
intro: This short diagnostic assesses your organization's current workforce planning maturity across six readiness areas. Answer honestly — there are no right or wrong answers. You'll receive a personalized maturity score, a domain-level breakdown, and tailored next steps via email.

domains.name: Workforce Planning Process
domains.description: How the organization currently plans staff capacity, shifts, on-call duties, clinical coverage, absences, overtime and schedule changes.

questions.text: Our workforce planning process is standardized and applied consistently across departments.
Is the workforce plan reviewed and updated on a regular, defined cadence?
How well are patient demand and staffing requirements connected in your current planning?
questions.note: Key readiness question for this domain.

questions.text: How effectively are schedule changes (last-minute swaps, absences, escalations) handled?
Is workforce planning primarily system-supported, as opposed to manual / Excel-based?

domains.name: Demand & Workload Planning
domains.description: How patient demand drivers (appointments, procedures, lab samples, peaks, no-shows, seasonality) are translated into required workforce capacity.

questions.text: How clearly does your organization translate patient demand into required staffing capacity?
questions.note: Key readiness question for this domain.

questions.text: Do you forecast demand drivers (appointments, procedures, lab/diagnostic volumes) systematically?
How well does planning account for peak periods, urgent cases, and no-shows / cancellations?
How accurately are standard durations defined for examinations, procedures and other activities?
Are seasonal patterns reflected in workforce demand forecasts?

domains.name: Staff Capacity & Availability
domains.description: Visibility into real effective capacity — not just headcount — including working hours, absences, training, part-time, shift patterns and role-specific availability.

questions.text: Does management know how much effective capacity is actually available by day, shift, role, location and department?
questions.note: Key readiness question for this domain.

questions.text: Is employee availability data (vacations, sick leave, training, part-time) reliable and up-to-date?
How well do you track shift patterns, location constraints and role-specific availability?
Is overtime tracked and visible in close-to-real time?
How accurate is your view of effective working hours (vs. nominal contracted hours)?

domains.name: Skills, Certifications & Clinical Coverage
domains.description: Whether the right people with the right qualifications, certifications and seniority are available for each shift — not just enough headcount.

questions.text: Can the organization clearly identify whether each shift has the required clinical skill coverage?
questions.note: Key readiness question for this domain.

questions.text: Do you maintain an up-to-date skill and certification matrix for clinical staff?
How well do you track license validity and certification expirations?
How low is your dependency on a small number of critical individuals for key skills? (5 = very low dependency, 1 = high single-point-of-failure risk)
Are supervision requirements (junior/senior pairing) clearly defined and enforced?

domains.name: Planning Rules & Governance
domains.description: Clarity and codification of planning rules — minimum staffing, ratios, rest, overtime limits, on-call rules, escalation — and clear ownership of planning, data and skill matrix updates.

questions.text: Are your planning rules clear enough to be translated into a digital rule engine?
questions.note: Key readiness question for this domain.

questions.text: Are minimum staffing requirements and patient-to-staff ratios formally documented?
How consistently are overtime limits and minimum rest-time rules applied across the organization?
Is ownership of workforce planning, data quality and skill-matrix updates clearly assigned?
How mature is your continuous-improvement rhythm for planning rules and governance?

domains.name: Systems & Data Landscape
domains.description: Whether HR, payroll, time-and-attendance, scheduling, HIS, appointment and lab systems are available, accurate, structured and sufficiently connected to support an AI planning tool.

questions.text: Are the required data sources available, accurate, structured and sufficiently connected to support an AI workforce planning tool?
questions.note: Key readiness question for this domain.

questions.text: Do your HR, payroll, time-and-attendance and scheduling systems exchange data automatically?
How well is workforce data consolidated, as opposed to spread across Excel files and local trackers?
How would you rate the overall data quality (completeness, accuracy, timeliness) of your workforce data?
Is there a dashboard or single source of truth where management can see current capacity and risks?

maturityLevels.name: Reactive
maturityLevels.headline: Workforce planning is largely manual and reactive.
maturityLevels.description: Capacity, skill and rule risks are mostly identified after operational problems occur. There is significant opportunity to build planning discipline before any AI investment.
maturityLevels.recommendation: Start with quick wins: standardize Excel templates, clean availability data, update the skill matrix and document critical planning rules.

maturityLevels.name: Emerging
maturityLevels.headline: Some planning practices are in place but inconsistent.
maturityLevels.description: Planning differs by department, data lives in disconnected files, and rules are known informally. Foundational improvements are required before scaling.
maturityLevels.recommendation: Define a TO-BE workforce planning process, establish data ownership and create a planning rule catalogue.

maturityLevels.name: Defined
maturityLevels.headline: Planning processes are defined but not yet AI-ready.
maturityLevels.description: You have a workable planning rhythm, but data fragmentation, rule gaps or governance ambiguity will limit AI value.
maturityLevels.recommendation: Tighten data quality, codify rules into mandatory vs. preferred categories, and pilot a demand-to-capacity calculation model.

maturityLevels.name: Managed
maturityLevels.headline: Mature planning with clear governance — ready for AI augmentation.
maturityLevels.description: Your processes, data and rules are largely in place. You are well-positioned to pilot the AI Workforce Planning Scenario & Gap Analyzer on a focused department.
maturityLevels.recommendation: Define MVP scope, select a pilot department (radiology, lab, outpatient, diagnostic centre), and validate rules with management before build.

maturityLevels.name: AI-Enabled
maturityLevels.headline: High-maturity, data-driven planning. Ready to deploy.
maturityLevels.description: You have the operational discipline, data foundations and governance to deploy and scale an AI-enabled scenario and gap analyzer with high confidence.
maturityLevels.recommendation: Move to full implementation: data preparation, rule-engine configuration, scenario logic and pilot validation across a target department.
